era_context: Middle Bronze Age. The Old Babylonian period is in its final decades. Egypt is in the Second Intermediate Period. The Indus Valley Civilization is in its mature phase. Mycenaean Greece is in the Early Bronze Age. This is a period of significant political fragmentation and the emergence of new regional powers, particularly the Hittites in Anatolia.

events.title: Samsu-ditana's reign in final phase
events.description: Samsu-ditana, the last king of the Old Babylonian dynasty, was ruling during the final years of the dynasty's existence. The kingdom was under increasing pressure from external enemies and internal instability. The Hittites were expanding southward, and other regional powers were challenging Babylonian authority. Within a few years, the dynasty would collapse, ending nearly 300 years of Babylonian rule.
events.certainty_note: The dating of Samsu-ditana's reign is uncertain, with chronological systems differing by up to 100 years. This year is assigned by convention within the Middle Chronology.

events.title: Hittite military campaigns in Anatolia
events.description: The Hittites under Hantili I were conducting military campaigns to consolidate control over Anatolia and expand their territorial reach. These campaigns were part of the broader process of Hittite emergence as a major regional power. The Hittites were beginning to challenge the dominance of older powers like Babylon and Egypt in the Near East.

sources.name: Hittite royal inscriptions and later chronicles

events.certainty_note: The specific dates and details of Hittite military campaigns in this period are not well-documented in contemporary sources. The narrative is reconstructed from later Hittite texts and Egyptian records.

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization at peak development
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization was at its height during this period, with a sophisticated urban culture, extensive trade networks, and advanced technology. The civilization had developed a unique script, standardized weights and measures, and impressive architectural achievements. The society appears to have been relatively egalitarian, with no obvious monumental temples or palaces, suggesting a different form of social organization than contemporary civilizations.
events.certainty_note: The material culture of the Indus Valley Civilization is well-documented through archaeology. The specific dating of this year is based on radiocarbon and stratigraphic analysis.

disconfirming_evidence: The chronology of this period is heavily debated. The Low Chronology would place these events approximately 100 years later. The extent and nature of Hittite military campaigns in this specific year are not documented in contemporary sources. The social organization of the Indus Valley Civilization is debated; some scholars argue for a more hierarchical structure than the archaeological evidence suggests.
historiographic_note: This year is poorly documented in contemporary written sources. Our knowledge is based on administrative records from Babylon, archaeological evidence from the Indus Valley and Egypt, and later historical compilations. The historical record is fragmentary and heavily dependent on archaeological dating methods with inherent uncertainties. The record is biased toward literate, urban civilizations.

graph_edges.note: Samsu-ditana's reign ended with the collapse of the Old Babylonian dynasty
